blocks.text: Abstract—The aspect graph is basically a multiple-view representation of the designated three-dimensional (3D) object. In this paper, a complete algorithm is presented for constructing the aspect graph of general polyhedral objects from the perspective point of view. The aspect graph generation is based on a so-called visual event. These visual events will completely capture the way the appearance of an edge or a face can be occluded by other faces and edges in the model. All the visual events can be found for polyhedra as facial, EE, and EEE events. The facial event indicates the visibility transition of the faces, the EE event indicates the possible visibility variation of the occlusion of two edges, and the EEE event represents another visibility change, the consecutive occlusion among three edges. The system consists of the following five stages: (1) find the viewing cell for each planar face, EE event, and EEE event and then create the "parcellation graph"; (2) employ the parcellation graph to create a "face-level enumeration array" and "edge-level enumeration array"; (3) generate test points; (4) examine plausible cells that enclose a volume of viewing space; and (5) generate an explicit aspect graph structure. Finally, nine non-convex polyhedra are obtained in the experiments. The aspect graphs of these non-regular shaped objects can be easily generated by this

blocks.text: The effective object representation of the three-dimensional (3D) objects is an essential problem for the major research area of 3D object recognition. Three-dimensional object recognition is basically a matching problem between the two-dimensional (2D) image information and the prestored model. The prestored model can be either a 3D object model or multiple 2D views of the objects. Here, we introduce an algorithm to generate effective multiple 2D views of the 3D objects so that the recognition process is reduced to a much simpler 2D to 2D matching problem. The idea behind this approach is that if a stored view is similar to the image, the recognition process will be simple and fast. The set of 2D views, which represent the infinite number of possible views, need to be as small as possible to minimize the number of views that must be stored. Therefore, the views are generally taken to be topologically distinct.

blocks.text: The aspect graph representation combines an exact 3D model of an object with a set of generic views. Each generic view is associated with a viewing cell of space from which it can be seen. Thus, the aspect graph is a hybrid representation, which combines the completeness of a viewpoint-independent representation with the simplicity of a viewpoint-dependent representation. The aspect graph of an object is a graph structure in which (1) each node of the graph, or aspect, represents a topologically distinct view of the object as seen from some maximal, connected region of viewpoint space, and there is a node for each aspect; (2) each arc represents a visual event, which occurs at the transition from one region of the viewpoint space to a neighboring region, and there is an arc for each such transition.

blocks.text: In the second approach, the viewing space, as proposed by Koenderink and van Doorn, '' is partitioned according to the qualitative structure of the view, which is called the aspect. An aspect may be defined in one of several ways, depending on the application at hand. The aspect may be defined by the graph structure of the line drawing, by the set of visible faces, by the set of occluding edges, or by the other structure of the view. They describe a graph structure called the "visual potential" of the object. blocks.text: When dealing with the convex polyhedra, the parcellation of viewing space is entirely determined by the planes on which the face of the object lies. The major complication in handling the non-convex polyhedra is the possibility of self-occlusion. Stewman and Bowyer(9) introduced the auxiliary surfaces for parcellation of the viewing space of the non-convex polyhedra. An auxiliary plane is defined by the visual interaction of a vertex and an edge of the object. Wang and Freeman 10) followed the same algorithm and renamed the three parcellation surfaces as type A, type B, and type C surfaces. However, the parcellation surfaces (types B and C) are not sufficient for describing all EE and EEE event transitions. Here, we analyze all the possible occurrences of EE and EEE events and develop algorithms for constructing their corresponding viewing cells. Our approach is different from previous methods in developing a robust and comprehensive aspect graph generation algorithm.

blocks.text: One of the major steps in aspect graph generation is to find the occurrences of singularities. The singularities are determined by three basic events: facial events, EE events, and EEE events. The facial event occurs when the viewer changes his viewpoints and the entire face becomes invisible. Also, when a T-junction appears when edge A occludes edge B or not, the EE event occurs. When the EEE event occurs, there will be three edges which occlude one another. A detailed description about the three events will be described later. In this section, we develop several concepts for finding the viewing cell in 3D viewpoint space from which the specific event (facial or EE event) can be observed. The differences between our approaches and the others are that we do a complete categorization of different types of EE events and use them to handle more complex non-convex objects. In the next section, we study EEE events and add these events to our system. Before mentioning the facial and EE events, we define some phrases that will be used throughout this paper.

blocks.text: An expanding plane of one face will separate the space into two parts: one part on the positive side of the plane is called outside the plane, and the other part on the negative side of the plane is called inside the plane. Figure 1 shows the inside and outside parts of a plane. In a convex object case, the viewing cells can be obtained through cutting the viewing space by the basic plane. This convex case is simple because no occlusion occurs. For a convex object, the viewpoint lies outside a face indicating that the face is visible for this viewpoint, whereas for the non-convex object, it is not necessarily true. In the non-convex case, even if the viewpoint lies outside the face, the face may still be invisible because it may be occluded by another face of the object. Here we introduce a partitioning plane called an auxiliary plane (AP), and take it with BP to partition the viewing space of a non-convex object. Figure 2 shows the basic plane and the auxiliary plane.

blocks.text: A face can be visible, partly occluded, or invisible. In this paper, a facial event indicates the visibility changes of a face. The face event occurs when the face visibility changes from visible to partial occluded, or invisible. The T-junction is observed when the face is partially occluded. For the viewpoint in the viewing subspace of the face is completely visible or partially visible, whereas for a viewpoint in the viewing cell of the facial event, the face must be completely visible. Therefore, if the viewpoint is not in the viewing cell of the facial event, the face may still be partially visible. In the EE event analysis, we may find the subset of the viewing subspace of the facial event, where the face is partially visible.

blocks.text: The necessary condition for the existence of the EE event is that the two edges must not be parallel. Therefore, the parallel edge pairs generate no EE events and have no viewing cells. After finding the possible existence of the EE event, we check the single-face complete interception. If one edge is located completely in the subspace bounded by the intersecting planes of the other edge, the possible existence EE event is single-face complete interception.

In Fig. 15, we illustrate the different configurations of EE events and EEE events that occur for three edges (e,, e2, e3). Type I EEE event to type Il EEE event transition must go through the EEE singularity, whereas the transitions between the EEE event to the EE event may go through either the EEE singularity or the EE singularity (foreground or background). The EEE singularity illustrates three edges are projected to intercept at one single point.

blocks.text: There may be some viewing cells of EE events existing in one viewing subspace of the EE event. The EEE event occurs when the viewing subspace of one EE event overlaps with the viewing subspace of another EE event. Figure 22 shows an example for the viewing subspaces of EE events. Region A is the viewing subspace of the EE1,2 event and region B the viewing subspace of the EE 2.3 event. Region C is the intersection region of regions A and B, and it represents the viewing cell of the EEE1,2.3 event (el is the background edge, e2 the middle edge, and e3 the foreground edge). Not all intersecting viewing subspaces of EE events will produce an EEE event. The necessary conditions of an EEE event are: (1) the viewing subspaces of two EE events must have an intersection region; (2) the foreground or the background edges of one EE event and the background or the foreground edges of the other EE event are the same edge. blocks.text: The input to our algorithm is a standard boundary surface representation of a non-convex planar-face object. Each face is specified by a planar polygon, and each polygon is specified by an order list of vertices. The vertices around the edge of a face are specified in counter-clockwise order as seen from outside the object. This allows us to easily and unambiguously refer to the "inside" and "outside" of each plane which contains a face of an object. The detailed implementation of our system is illustrated as follows.

Each face is specified by a planar polygon, and each polygon is specified by an order list of vertices. The vertices around the edge of a face are specified in counter-clockwise order as seen from outside the object. This allows us to easily and unambiguously refer to the "inside" and "outside" of each plane which contains a face of an object. The detailed implementation of our system is illustrated as follows.

blocks.text: The first step in creating the aspect graph is to generate the intermediate parcellation graph data structure. The parcellation graph is a three-level bidirectional graph which summarizes the parcellation of space by generating the nodes that indicate the facial events, the EE events, and the EEE events. (1) Level l parcellation. The N possible facial events which are expanding planes (BP) in which convex (non-occluding) faces of the object lie that do not intercept any other faces and intersecting half-planes in which concave (occluding) faces may not be occluded. blocks.text: At the second level of the parcellation graph are nodes for all the possible EE events of the two edges of two non-neighboring faces. The inter-level link between the nodes of the first two levels indicate the relationship of the face and its bounding edges. The Name of the node indicates foreground-background edges and the Attribute of the node is the extended tetrahedron enclosed by four half-planes and the bounded tetrahedron surrounded by four triangles formed by the four terminals of the two edges. The Attribute indicates the possible viewing cell that may capture the occlusion event of the two edges. The level II nodes are identified by non-interception EE event finding criteria. If the level Il nodes have the same foreground edge, then they are linked to the same level Ill node. The two background edges of those level II nodes may also make occlusion with each other and create other EEE events. This level III node, therefore, may have inter-level links to three level II nodes. At the third level of the parcellation graph are nodes for all the possible EEE events of three edges of three non-adjacent faces. The relation between EE nodes and EEE nodes will be generated after step (d). Figure 23 shows the structure of the parcellation graph.

blocks.text: For creating the test points, we use multiple Gaussian spheres, which have their common center located on the centroids of viewed objects, to generate a set of test points. The Gaussian spheres with different radius are used for generating the test points which are evenly distributed on the spheres. We let the number of test points on each Gaussian sphere be the same. In other words, for each small solid angle from the centroid of the object, there are a set of test points with different distances from the object. The density of these test points is determined by the complexity of the object, as long as no aspect is missed between two test points. In Fig. 25, we illustrate the Gaussian sphere from which the test points are selected for a polyhedron.

blocks.text: We apply our algorithm to various different objects and the shapes of those objects are not regular. The faces of the object may be a triangle or a polygon, also they may be inclined. In this paper, we strictly analyze all the relations between each edge pair and classify all the edge pairs into several edge types. We use occlusion type, interception type, and group 1 or group 2 to classify the edge pairs. Through the edge type categorization, all the important aspects of polyhedra will be generated. We can handle more complex non-convex polyhedra. Furthermore, we also investigate the EEE event, the occurrence condition and the transition between EEE events.

blocks.text: About the Author -CHUNG-LIn HuanG was born in Taichung, Taiwan, in 1955. He received his B.S. degree in nuclear engineering from National Tsing-Hua University, Hsin-Chu, Taiwan, in 1977, and M.S. degree in electrical engineering from National Taiwan University, Taipei, Taiwan, in 1979, respectively. He obtained his Ph.D. degree in electrical engineering from University of Florida, Gainesville, Florida, U.S.A., in 1987. From 1981 to 1983, he was an associate engineer in ERSO, ITRI, Hsin-Chu, Taiwan. From 1987 to 1988, he worked for the Unisys Co., Orange County, California, U.S.A., as a project engineer. Since August 1988 he has been with the Electrical Engineering Department, National Tsing-Hua University as an associate professor. His research interests are in the areas of image processing, computer vision, and visual communication. Dr Huang is a member of IEEE and SPIE.
